title: Planning with AI
intro: You're about to use AI to help plan your task manager. It's like hiring a brilliant but literal-minded assistant — give clear instructions and you get great results, but be vague and you get chaos. The decisions you make here will shape the entire project. Choose wisely.

teach.title: AI Is Your Junior Developer
teach.body: Think of AI as a very fast, very eager junior developer. It will do exactly what you ask — which means if you ask poorly, you get poor results. Your job is to be the **senior developer**: set the direction, define the constraints, and review the output critically.

teach.title: The GPS Analogy
teach.body: AI is like a GPS: it can find the route, but YOU need to know the destination. If you type in the wrong address, the GPS will confidently drive you to the wrong place. Same with AI — it will confidently build the wrong thing if your prompt is wrong.

teach.title: Directing a Movie
teach.body: You're the director, AI is the crew. A great crew can build sets, film scenes, and edit footage — but without a director's vision, they'll produce a random mess. Your job isn't to hold the camera. It's to say 'here's the story we're telling, here's the mood, here's the ending.' Give AI the vision, and it'll execute beautifully.

teach.title: Structuring an AI Planning Session
teach.code:
Step 1: Define the goal
  "I'm building a task manager for personal use"

Step 2: List the features (from your user stories)
  "Core features: create, complete, delete, due dates"

Step 3: Set constraints
  "Tech: Next.js + Supabase. Mobile-first. Ship in 2 weeks."

Step 4: Ask for a plan, not code
  "Give me a project plan with milestones, not code yet"
teach.annotation: Notice: you're asking for a PLAN first, not jumping straight to code. This is what separates thoughtful builders from chaotic ones.

teach.body: Professional developers charge $150+/hour for project planning. You're learning to do this with AI as your co-pilot — a skill that lets you plan and build entire products solo, keeping all the profit for yourself.

Use AI for planning BEFORE coding. Ask it to poke holes in your plan, suggest what you might be missing, and identify risks. But always review its suggestions critically — you're the architect, it's the assistant.

start.narrative: You open your AI assistant to plan the task manager project. You have your feature list and user stories ready. How do you begin the planning session?

choices.text: "Generate a Next.js task manager app with Supabase"
"I'm building a task manager. Here are my features: [list]. Help me create a project plan with milestones."
"What should I build? Give me a project idea"

code-first.narrative: The AI generates 500 lines of code. It works, sort of — but the data model doesn't match your feature list, there's no auth, and the UI is generic. You've skipped planning and now you're debugging someone else's architecture decisions. What do you do?

choices.text: Scrap the generated code, paste your real feature list back into the chat, and ask for a milestone plan instead
Try to fix the generated code piece by piece without going back to planning

plan-first.narrative: The AI suggests a 4-milestone plan: 1) Setup + data model, 2) Core CRUD UI, 3) Filtering + sorting, 4) Polish + deploy. It also flags a question: 'Will users need accounts, or is this local-only?' How do you handle this?

choices.text: "Users need accounts — we'll use Supabase Auth. Add a milestone 0 for auth setup."
"Skip auth for now, we'll add it later"

ai-decides.narrative: The AI suggests a recipe app, a habit tracker, and a budget tool. None of these are what you wanted. You realize you let the AI lead instead of leading it. What now?

choices.text: "Actually, I already know what I'm building. Here's my feature list and user stories — let's plan from this."
Pick the habit tracker because it sounds fun and pivot the whole project

review-plan.narrative: You now have a draft milestone plan from the AI. Before you treat it as gospel, how do you review it?

choices.text: Ask the AI to critique its own plan: 'What's missing? What could go wrong? Where am I being unrealistic?'
Skim the milestones, fix anything that looks obviously wrong, and move on
Trust the plan as-is — the AI is smarter than you about software

scope-risks.narrative: The plan is reviewed. The AI flags three risks: scope creep (your feature list is large), no time budgeted for testing, and an ambitious 2-week deadline. How do you address scope before writing any code?

choices.text: Mark each feature as 'MVP' or 'V2'. Anything not strictly required to ship a usable task manager moves to V2.
Keep every feature but extend the deadline from 2 weeks to 6 weeks
Leave the feature list as-is and trust that you'll move fast enough

first-step.narrative: Plan is scoped, risks are noted. Now you have to pick the very first concrete task to start building. What do you ask the AI for?

choices.text: "Walk me through the data model first: tables, columns, and relationships for tasks and users."
"Generate the homepage UI so I have something visible to show."
"Now generate all the code for milestone 1 in one go."

excellent-end.narrative: You start with the data model — the foundation everything else sits on. The AI lays out tables, columns, and relationships. You spot two missing fields before any code is written and fix them in seconds. You now have a roadmap, scoped MVP, and a solid foundation to build on.
excellent-end.feedback: This is how senior developers use AI: bring your own vision, plan before coding, ask the AI to critique its own work, ruthlessly scope to MVP, and build foundations before features. You stayed in the driver's seat the whole way.

mediocre-end.narrative: You start with the homepage UI. It looks great, but two days later you realize the mockup assumed a data shape your tables don't support. You rework the UI to match the real data, losing time you didn't have to spare.
mediocre-end.feedback: Starting with UI feels productive because you see results fast, but it locks in assumptions about data that may not hold. Build the data model first — the UI is much easier to redo than the schema.

weak-end.narrative: You ask the AI to generate everything for milestone 1 at once. It produces hundreds of lines of code that don't quite fit together. You spend the rest of the week debugging integration issues that careful step-by-step planning would have prevented.
weak-end.feedback: Big-bang code generation defeats the point of planning. AI works best when you feed it one bounded task at a time. Generate the schema, review it, generate one component, review it — small loops keep you in control.
